params.text:
Supporting choice and control

NICE guidance is built on choice, independence and control. People have the right to make decisions about their own lives — big and small — and to be supported to make changes if they wish.

To support real choice you should:

  • Offer genuine, realistic options and enough information to decide.
  • Make reasonable adjustments and use accessible communication so the choice is truly the person’s own.
  • Respect decisions even when you would choose differently — this includes the right to take positive risks.
  • Explain honestly if a preference cannot be met, and look for alternatives together.

Offering someone a choice of drink, what to wear, or when to get up may seem minor, but these everyday choices are exactly where control and dignity are won or lost.

params.alt: Small comforts matter — a cup of tea

params.question: Mrs Okafor wants to wear her favourite skirt and cardigan today, but they are a little creased. What best reflects choice and dignity?

answers.text: Support her choice and help her wear the outfit she has chosen.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct. Respecting everyday choices about clothing supports her identity, dignity and control.

answers.text: Choose a different outfit for her because the creased one looks untidy.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: This overrides her choice based on your preference, not hers. It undermines her dignity and control.

answers.text: Tell her residents all wear the same clothes provided by the service.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: This is a 'one size fits all' approach, the opposite of person-centred care.

answers.text: Refuse to help until she agrees to a 'smarter' outfit.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Making support conditional on her changing her mind removes her right to choose.
